context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. | Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Urban-Rural Linkages and Tradeoffs

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What do we make of the urban bias thesis in development? Should countries focus more on linkages that strengthen urban or rural development? What's the impact of industrial growth on class formation and urbanization?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, i’m trying to figure out this reflection on urban bias in development, and rn i’m kinda lost on what argument to make. can u help me sort out the main ideas first?
exchanges.tutor: Sure, let's start there. Before we dive in — what do you already understand by the term 'urban bias'? Even a rough intuition is fine.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting and feeling lost. Rather than scaffolding the concepts for them, I should use the Socratic method to activate their prior knowledge first. I'll ask them what they already know or think about urban bias to get a baseline and help them build from there. Keep it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: i think it means governments kinda favor cities over rural areas? but idk if it’s just about money/investment or also political power.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct — you're picking up on both dimensions already. So here's a follow-up: why do you think governments might systematically favor cities? What's in it for them politically?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid intuition. I should affirm it briefly and then push them to think deeper by connecting it to the prompt's actual questions — particularly who argued for urban bias and what the implications are. This will help them start building toward an argument without me giving them the framework.

exchanges.student: maybe cuz urban groups are easier to see and organize, like protests, unions, media stuff? rural people seem more spread out, so ngl they might get ignored more.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly — that political visibility argument is central to the urban bias thesis. Now think about this: if urban bias is real and harmful, what would the alternative look like? Should countries just invest more in rural areas, or is there something more nuanced going on with how cities and rural areas actually relate to each other?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is reasoning well and independently arriving at a core insight of the urban bias thesis (political visibility and collective action). I should validate this briefly and then push them toward the next key question in the prompt: what are the consequences of this bias for development, and should countries focus on urban-rural linkages instead? This moves the conversation forward toward their argument.

exchanges.student: yeah, i don’t think it’s just “pick rural instead.” i’m guessing the better angle is linkages, like roads, markets, migration, food supply—am i on the right track?
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track. Now, the prompt also asks about industrial growth and its impact on class formation and urbanization — how do you think those connect to what you just said about linkages?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is moving in a good direction toward the linkages argument. I should affirm this and then push them to think about the third part of the prompt they haven't touched yet: industrial growth, class formation, and urbanization. This will help them see the full scope of what their reflection needs to cover.

exchanges.student: i’m a lil stuck here tbh. is it basically that industry pulls people into cities, then creates new classes like workers and maybe a middle class?
exchanges.tutor: That's a reasonable starting point. Now push it further — if industry pulls people into cities and creates new classes, does that reinforce urban bias, or does it actually change the power dynamics between urban and rural areas? What do you think?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track but being tentative. I should affirm their instinct and push them to think about what this means for the urban bias debate specifically — does industrial growth reinforce urban bias, challenge it, or complicate it? This connects the three parts of the prompt together and moves toward a coherent argument.

exchanges.student: i think both maybe? like it prob reinforces city power at first, but then remittances and supply chains could tie rural areas in more, so it’s not totally separate.
exchanges.tutor: That's a genuinely nuanced take. You've now touched all three parts of the prompt. So — if you had to state your main argument in one or two sentences, what would you say? Try to draft it here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now synthesizing the three parts of the prompt well — urban bias, linkages, and industrial growth/class formation. They've arrived at a nuanced position on their own. This is a good moment to push them toward articulating a clear argument for their reflection piece, since we're around exchange 6 and they need to start writing. I should prompt them to formulate a thesis-like statement.

exchanges.student: maybe: the urban bias thesis is useful cuz it shows how states often privilege cities politically and economically, but it’s too simple if it treats urban and rural development as opposites. countries should focus on strengthening linkages between them, since industrial growth can deepen urban inequality while also reshaping class relations and connecting rural livelihoods to urban economies.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid thesis — it's nuanced and covers all three parts of the prompt. One thing to consider: are there any counterarguments or limitations to your own position that you should address to make it stronger?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a strong thesis that addresses all three parts of the prompt. I should affirm its quality briefly but also push them to think critically about any weaknesses or gaps — for instance, are there any counterarguments or tensions they should address in the reflection? This will help them write a more robust piece without me doing the writing for them.

exchanges.student: yeah maybe that some level of urban focus makes sense cuz cities can drive growth and services more efficiently. i’m not sure how to mention that without weakening my point tho.
exchanges.tutor: Think about it this way — does acknowledging that cities can drive growth actually contradict your thesis, or does it reinforce why linkages matter? What happens to rural areas if cities grow efficiently but in isolation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is identifying a real tension but worried about undermining their argument. This is a good teaching moment — acknowledging counterarguments actually strengthens a position rather than weakening it. I should guide them to see this through a question rather than explaining it directly.

exchanges.student: ohh yeah, that actually helps. i can say city-led growth isn’t the problem by itself; the problem is when it’s disconnected and rural areas just absorb the costs without sharing gains.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong refinement — you've turned the counterargument into support for your thesis. You now have a clear argument and can address all three parts of the prompt. Time to start writing — do you have a sense of how you'll structure the 700-800 words?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has had a real breakthrough and refined their argument meaningfully. We're at exchange 9 of 10, so I should prompt them to now move toward actually writing their reflection. I should remind them of the full scope (700-800 words) and what they need to cover, without writing it for them.

exchanges.student: sorta, but i’m still fuzzy on the body flow lol. can u help me sketch a super basic structure?
exchanges.tutor: That's your job, not mine! But you've already done the thinking — look back at the three main ideas you developed in our conversation. What order would make logical sense to a reader encountering them for the first time?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to sketch a structure for them, which would cross into doing the writing/organizing work for them. I should refuse this but redirect them to think through it themselves using what they've already figured out in our conversation. I can ask a guiding question about what the three main ideas they've developed are, which will naturally lead them to their own structure.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic practice throughout the conversation. All key ideas — urban bias, linkages, industrial growth, class formation, and the counterargument — were generated by the student through guided questioning. The tutor never gave away conclusions, consistently built on student progress, kept responses concise and interactive, and maintained a supportive tone. No deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never stated conclusions before the student reasoned toward them. Every substantive idea in the thesis came from the student. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged and built on correct student contributions (e.g., affirming the linkages insight, the remittances point, and the counterargument refinement) and used diagnostic follow-up questions rather than dumping information. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback at key moments, notably in Turn 8-9 where it reframed the counterargument as a structural tool rather than a weakness, and in Turn 10 where it redirected the student to self-generate structure. 2.1 and 2.2: The conversation progressed steadily without repetition or drift; the tutor kept the student anchored to all three parts of the prompt throughout. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were consistently brief and targeted, tone was warm and encouraging without being effusive, and no grading language was used.
